entities.name: glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonists
entities.context: Emerging evidence suggests that glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonists (GLP-1RAs), developed for diabetes and obesity, may offer novel analgesia and treat drug-seeking behavior.

entities.name: Chronic Pain
entities.context: Chronic pain imposes a significant healthcare burden, with treatments often limited by side effects, opioid dependency, and preventable surgeries.

entities.name: Osteoarthritis
entities.context: In osteoarthritis, they appear to reduce inflammation and cartilage degradation, but trials in non-obese, non-comorbid patients are needed.

entities.name: Diabetic Neuropathy
entities.context: In diabetic neuropathy, GLP-1RAs show potential for nerve repair, but optimal dosing and long-term efficacy need clarification.

entities.name: Migraine
entities.context: Preclinical data support GLP-1RAs signaling in migraines, but human studies are lacking.

entities.name: Alcohol Use Disorder
entities.context: Trials in alcohol addiction show promise, though evidence for other substances remains inconclusive.

entities.name: Substance Use Disorder
entities.context: This review examines the role of GLP-1RAs in pain management, focusing on inflammation, macrophage repolarization, oxidative stress, and dopaminergic pathways in substance use disorders.

entities.name: Diabetes Mellitus
entities.context: Emerging evidence suggests that glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonists (GLP-1RAs), developed for diabetes and obesity, may offer novel analgesia and treat drug-seeking behavior.

entities.name: Obesity
entities.context: Emerging evidence suggests that glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonists (GLP-1RAs), developed for diabetes and obesity, may offer novel analgesia and treat drug-seeking behavior.

entities.name: Opioid Dependence
entities.context: GLP-1RAs offer a promising shift in pain management, potentially reducing opioid dependence, preventing surgical interventions, and lowering healthcare costs.

entities.name: GLP-1 receptor agonism
entities.context: These medications act on various parts of the body, including the brain and nervous system. They may reduce inflammation, protect nerve cells, and influence how the brain perceives pain or reacts to addictive substances.

entities.name: GLP-1 receptor
entities.context: glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonists (GLP-1RAs), developed for diabetes and obesity, may offer novel analgesia and treat drug-seeking behavior.

entities.name: macrophage repolarization
entities.context: This review examines the role of GLP-1RAs in pain management, focusing on inflammation, macrophage repolarization, oxidative stress, and dopaminergic pathways in substance use disorders.

entities.name: oxidative stress
entities.context: This review examines the role of GLP-1RAs in pain management, focusing on inflammation, macrophage repolarization, oxidative stress, and dopaminergic pathways in substance use disorders.

entities.name: dopaminergic pathway
entities.context: This review examines the role of GLP-1RAs in pain management, focusing on inflammation, macrophage repolarization, oxidative stress, and dopaminergic pathways in substance use disorders.

entities.name: opioids
entities.context: current treatments like opioids often come with serious risks, including addiction.

entities.name: Bade Sahil et al., Pain Management 2025
entities.context: GLP-1 agonists: a game changer in pain treatment and addiction. Pain management (2025). PMID: 40726115.

relations.evidence: glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonists (GLP-1RAs), developed for diabetes and obesity, may offer novel analgesia and treat drug-seeking behavior.
glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonists (GLP-1RAs), developed for diabetes and obesity, may offer novel analgesia and treat drug-seeking behavior.
Emerging evidence suggests that glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonists (GLP-1RAs), developed for diabetes and obesity, may offer novel analgesia and treat drug-seeking behavior.
GLP-1RAs offer a promising shift in pain management, potentially reducing opioid dependence, preventing surgical interventions, and lowering healthcare costs.
Trials in alcohol addiction show promise, though evidence for other substances remains inconclusive.
In osteoarthritis, they appear to reduce inflammation and cartilage degradation, but trials in non-obese, non-comorbid patients are needed.
In diabetic neuropathy, GLP-1RAs show potential for nerve repair, but optimal dosing and long-term efficacy need clarification.
Preclinical data support GLP-1RAs signaling in migraines, but human studies are lacking.

These medications act on various parts of the body, including the brain and nervous system. They may reduce inflammation, protect nerve cells, and influence how the brain perceives pain or reacts to addictive substances.
glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonists (GLP-1RAs), developed for diabetes and obesity, may offer novel analgesia and treat drug-seeking behavior.
This review examines the role of GLP-1RAs in pain management, focusing on inflammation, macrophage repolarization, oxidative stress, and dopaminergic pathways in substance use disorders.

Many people live with chronic pain, and current treatments like opioids often come with serious risks, including addiction.
GLP-1RAs offer a promising shift in pain management, potentially reducing opioid dependence, preventing surgical interventions, and lowering healthcare costs.
